original_hypothesis.title: Permissionless initProject Allows Admin Hijacking of Token Pair Projects
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.initProject() is permissionless (no access control beyond afterInitialize). It sets msg.sender as the project admin for a Uniswap V3 pool address derived from the token pair. An attacker who knows a project's planned sale/raise token pair can call initProject first, becoming the admin. As admin, they can initialize ILO pools with malicious vesting configs (e.g., assigning 99% of liquidity shares to themselves), control the whitelist, and receive project refunds.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: If the attacker can observe token pair info (from announcements, token deployments, or configuration transactions), they can preemptively claim admin rights. This doesn't require mempool observation — just public information about planned launches.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No access control on initProject(); _project.admin = msg.sender; first caller wins for any token pair

preconditions: Attacker knows or guesses the saleToken/raiseToken/fee combination
No project has been initialized for that token pair yet

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: admin = msg.sender without authorization; uniV3Pool address deterministic from token pair
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call initProject with target token pair before legitimate project; gain admin of all ILO pools for that project
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Create ILO pools with vesting configs giving attacker 99% of project shares

economic_questions: How discoverable are token pair plans before initProject is called?
What value can the hijacked admin extract via malicious vesting configs?
Can the attacker also DoS the legitimate project permanently?
validation_requirements: Confirm initProject has no access control
Test malicious vesting config deployment after admin hijack

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Only gas cost for initProject call (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 99% of project's liquidity shares if ILO proceeds
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Extremely high if legitimate investors deposit into attacker-controlled pool
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction to hijack; persistent control thereafter

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Before legitimate project team calls initProject
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Legitimate project permanently locked out of their token pair

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Legitimate project admin and all investors
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Potential total loss if investing into attacker-controlled pool

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm admin hijack gives control over initILOPool vesting configs

validation_result.impact_summary: Any caller can claim admin rights over a token pair's project by calling initProject first, gaining control over ILO pool creation (vesting configs), whitelist management, and project refunds.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ILOManager.initProject() function has no access control beyond the afterInitialize modifier (which only checks that the contract itself is initialized). Inside _cacheProject, msg.sender is assigned as the project admin. The require(_project.uniV3PoolAddress == address(0), 'RE') check enforces first-caller-wins semantics. Since Uniswap V3 pool addresses are deterministic from (saleToken, raiseToken, fee), an attacker who knows the planned token pair can call initProject before the legitimate project team, permanently claiming admin rights. As admin, the attacker can call initILOPool with malicious vesting configs (e.g., assigning 99% of liquidity shares to their own addresses as project recipients), control the whitelist to allow/deny participation, and claim project refunds. The legitimate project team cannot overwrite the admin because of the 'RE' check. This is a clear access control defect - a function that assigns a privileged role (project admin) lacks authorization checks.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: ILOManager.sol initProject() at approximately lines 47-54 has only the afterInitialize() modifier. _cacheProject() sets _project.admin = msg.sender with the only guard being require(_project.uniV3PoolAddress == address(0), 'RE') which is a first-caller-wins pattern. The onlyProjectAdmin modifier gates initILOPool, transferAdminProject, claimRefund, and ILOPool whitelist management, all of which become attacker-controlled.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No access control (e.g., onlyOwner) on initProject. Any address can call it and become the project admin for a given token pair.

_project.admin = msg.sender assigns the caller as admin. The require check only prevents re-initialization (first-caller-wins). No verification that msg.sender is authorized to create a project for this token pair.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls ILOManager.initProject({saleToken, raiseToken, fee, initialPoolPriceX96, launchTime})
3. PoolAddress.getPoolKey computes deterministic pool key from token pair + fee
4. _initUniV3PoolIfNecessary creates or verifies Uniswap V3 pool, returns deterministic address
6. require(_project.uniV3PoolAddress == address(0), 'RE') prevents legitimate team from ever overwriting
7. Attacker now calls initILOPool (gated by onlyProjectAdmin) with malicious vesting configs
8. Attacker controls whitelist via ILOPool.batchWhitelist/setOpenToAll (gated by onlyProjectAdmin)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: initProject() -> afterInitialize() (passes if contract initialized) -> _cacheProject() sets admin=msg.sender with no authorization check. The onlyProjectAdmin modifier at ILOManager.sol line 46 then gates all subsequent admin operations (initILOPool, transferAdminProject, claimRefund) to the attacker's address.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts (Deploy.s.sol, Init.s.sol) show ILOManager is initialized with an owner and fee configuration, but initProject access control is not configured or restricted by any deployment parameter. The scripts do not add any onlyOwner or allowlist restriction to initProject.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is fully viable: (1) Attacker observes publicly announced token pair (saleToken + raiseToken), which is commonly known before ILO launch from project announcements, token contract deployments, or even from observing the Uniswap V3 factory. (2) Attacker calls initProject with the target token pair before the legitimate project team. (3) The 'RE' check permanently prevents the legitimate team from claiming admin. (4) Attacker calls initILOPool with vesting configs giving 99% to their own address. (5) If investors participate (not realizing the admin is malicious), the attacker extracts the majority of liquidity shares. Even without investor participation, the legitimate project is permanently DoS'd for that token pair + fee combination.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. The legitimate project team is permanently locked out from using their intended token pair (with that specific fee tier). If investors participate in attacker-controlled pools, the attacker can configure vesting to extract up to 99% of project liquidity shares. The attacker also controls whitelist (can block/allow arbitrary addresses) and can claim project refunds. This is an elevation of privilege vulnerability that undermines the entire project admin model.
recommended_mitigations: Add onlyOwner modifier to initProject, requiring the ILOManager owner to approve project creation
Alternatively, implement an allowlist of approved project creators that the owner manages
Consider a two-step project creation: owner approves a (tokenPair, admin) mapping, then the designated admin calls initProject
Add a mechanism for the ILOManager owner to override or reset project admin in case of front-running
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ILOManager and initialize it. 2. From attacker address, call initProject with a target token pair. 3. Verify _cachedProject[uniV3Pool].admin == attacker. 4. From legitimate team address, attempt initProject with same pair — expect revert 'RE'. 5. From attacker, call initILOPool with vesting configs assigning 99% shares to attacker-controlled recipient. 6. Verify the ILO pool is created with attacker's vesting config.
uncertainties: In practice, how quickly would investors realize the pool admin is not the legitimate project team?
Projects could potentially use a different fee tier to work around the hijack, but this forces a non-optimal pool configuration
additional_testing_needed: Test whether the Uniswap V3 pool price requirement in _initUniV3PoolIfNecessary creates any additional friction for the attacker
Test whether the attacker can also DoS multiple fee tiers simultaneously for the same token pair
Verify that transferAdminProject cannot be used by any party to reclaim admin from the attacker
